title: practical english usage - Part 113
content:
Anna's in the kitchen. I can't find the car papers.

Could you close the door? (Only one door is open.) c   it is clear from the situation which  one(s) we  mean He's already lost the phone he bought last week.

Tell Amy  the story about Jack and Susie.

Who are the girls over there with Tom?

b  we  say which  one(s) we  mean (The listener has already heard about the money and the policeman.) 'So what did you do then?’ 'Gave the money right back to the policeman.' She's got two children: a boy and a girl. The boy's fourteen and the girl's eight.

a  we  have mentioned it/them before Our listener/reader may know which  one(s) we  mean because: I don't drink coffee, (any coffee) - What did you do with the coffee I bought? (The speaker says which  coffee.) I need to buy clothes. (No one knows exactly which  clothes will be bought.) - Did you wash the clothes? (The listener knows which  clothes.) She arrived in an old taxi. (The speaker doesn’ t say which  old taxi.) - She arrived on the 8.15 train. (The speaker says which  train.) Let's go and see a film. (The speaker doesn’ t say which  one.) - I didn't like the film, (the one that the speaker and listener saw) Is there a bank near here? (any bank) - I'm going to the bank. (The listener knows which: the usual one.) Compare: easily see) which  particular person(s), thing(s), etc we  are talking about.

noun (singular, plural or uncountable) when our listener/reader knows (or can The usually means something like 'you know which  ɪ  mean’ . We   use the before a 1 the    = 'you   know     which     one(s)' 136 more    about     the For more detailed information about articles, see the following sections.

Annie is a doctor, (    Annie-is-doeforz) not Give it to the cat. (     Give it to cat.) every cat, but not just cat. (For exceptions, ►     142.) determiner. We   can say a cat, the cat, my cat, this cat, any cat, either cat or •    Don’ t use singular countable nouns alone, without an article or other not this problem (     the-this problem) not a friend of mine (     a my friend) not my work (     the my work) •    Don’ t use articles together with my, this, or other determiners.

Gas is expensive, (     The gas...) not Elephants can swim very well, (     The elephants ean swim ...) (For exceptions, ►     140.2.) •    Don’ t use the to talk about things in general. The does not mean ‘ all’ .

grammar •  136   more about the The is normally pronounced /ði:/  before a vowel and /də/   before a consonant.  -» 8  pronunciation not Life is hard, (     The life is hard.) not Books are expensive, (     The books arc expensive.) mean 'all'. (For details and exceptions, ►     140.) We   usually use no article, not the, to talk about things in general - the does not 7 things     in  general 'My name's Emma  Watson.' 'What, not the Emma  Watson?

‘ the well-known'.

But note the use of the (pronounced /ði:/) with a person's name to mean not Emily lives in Switzerland, (     The Emily lives in-the Switzerland.) exceptions, ►     142.18-142.19).

We   do not usually use the with singular proper nouns (there are some 6 proper     nouns     (names) not I like this beer, (     .. .-1 he-th is-beer.) not Is that Emily's car? (     .. .-the-EmUy^s-e&r?) not This is my uncle, (     ... the my uncle.) We   do not use the with possessives or demonstratives.

5 possessives         and   demonstratives I'd like you to meet Cathy Parker, the novelist.

She married Brad Pitt, the actor.

that the person referred to is 'the well-known one’ .

After a name, an identifying expression with the is often used to make it clear the 4    meaning       'the   well-known' We went to the same school.

I'm the oldest in my family.  Can I have the next pancake?

and only.

talking about). For the same reason, we  usually use the with first, next, last, same one best, biggest, etc individual or group (so it is clear which  one(s) we  are We   usually use the with superlatives (►   208.6) because there is normally only the    oldest.

3 superlatives:        /'m but has enough information to identify it.) the supermarket. (The listener doesn't know the supermarket, Go straight over two sets of traffic lights and then turn right after one family of that name around.) Do you know the Aldersons? (It is clear to the listener that there can only be People used to think the earth was flat.
